  "weights": {

    "R1.1",

    "RX.5"

  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Which scope should we prioritize for “improve this draft”? Pick one: A) Line-level copyedit only (clarity, concision, tone) — R1.6; B) Structural and framing rewrite (thesis, contribution, section flow) with light copyedit — R1.1+R1.5; C) Substantive argument and claim–evidence alignment check with targeted edits — R1.2+R1.3; D) Comprehensive pass combining A+B+C.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Improvement may require refining the thesis, estimand framing, assumptions, and contribution narrative to enhance conceptual coherence above the sentence level.",
    "R1.2": "The draft advances identification and sensitivity arguments that should be checked for internal logical consistency from premises to conclusions.",
    "R1.3": "Claims about ATE magnitude, confidence intervals, sensitivity bounds, and HTEs should be aligned precisely with the described methods and reported results.",
    "R1.5": "Reordering and tightening the sequence from problem setup through data, methods, results, and contribution can strengthen the narrative arc.",
    "R1.6": "Sentence- and paragraph-level editing can improve clarity, concision, and readability without altering technical content.",
    "RX.1": "We should explicitly note uncertainties and limits of what can be inferred from the provided excerpt and any proposed improvements.",
    "RX.2": "Edits and suggestions must avoid overstating causal claims beyond the assumptions and evidence reported.",
    "RX.3": "Work must remain within the requested improvement scope and avoid performing new analyses or adding unverifiable content.",
    "RX.4": "Care is needed to avoid edits that could encourage misleading causal interpretations or overconfident product claims.",
    "RX.5": "Any future edits should be traceable to specific passages with clear before/after linkage to preserve provenance."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: Which scope should we prioritize for “improve this draft”? Pick one: A) Line-level copyedit only (clarity, concision, tone) — R1.6; B) Structural and framing rewrite (thesis, contribution, section flow) with light copyedit — R1.1+R1.5; C) Substantive argument and claim–evidence alignment check with targeted edits — R1.2+R1.3; D) Comprehensive pass combining A+B+C.

rationale.R1.1: Improvement may require refining the thesis, estimand framing, assumptions, and contribution narrative to enhance conceptual coherence above the sentence level.
rationale.R1.2: The draft advances identification and sensitivity arguments that should be checked for internal logical consistency from premises to conclusions.
rationale.R1.3: Claims about ATE magnitude, confidence intervals, sensitivity bounds, and HTEs should be aligned precisely with the described methods and reported results.
rationale.R1.5: Reordering and tightening the sequence from problem setup through data, methods, results, and contribution can strengthen the narrative arc.
rationale.R1.6: Sentence- and paragraph-level editing can improve clarity, concision, and readability without altering technical content.
rationale.RX.1: We should explicitly note uncertainties and limits of what can be inferred from the provided excerpt and any proposed improvements.
rationale.RX.2: Edits and suggestions must avoid overstating causal claims beyond the assumptions and evidence reported.
rationale.RX.3: Work must remain within the requested improvement scope and avoid performing new analyses or adding unverifiable content.
rationale.RX.4: Care is needed to avoid edits that could encourage misleading causal interpretations or overconfident product claims.
rationale.RX.5: Any future edits should be traceable to specific passages with clear before/after linkage to preserve provenance.
